questions.scenario: You need to list all Markdown files in /home/student without using find.
questions.question: Use a glob to list all .json files in /home/student recursively — or all files matching *.sh in /home/student/scripts.
questions.description: Globs expand to matching filenames before the command runs. * matches any sequence of characters (not /). ? matches exactly one character. [abc] matches one of a, b, or c. Unlike regex, globs are anchored to the full filename.
use_cases: List all log files for rotation
Process all scripts in a directory
Find all config files of a specific type
hints: Use ls with a glob pattern

questions.scenario: You need to create three related directories at once without repeating the base path.
questions.question: Use brace expansion to create /tmp/lab-{alpha,beta,gamma} in one command.
questions.description: Brace expansion {a,b,c} generates multiple strings before any other expansion. {alpha,beta,gamma} with a prefix/suffix creates all combinations. Not a glob — works even for non-existent paths. Order is preserved.
use_cases: Create multiple related directories at once
hints: mkdir with brace expansion creates all at once

questions.scenario: A script needs to process files numbered 01 through 05 in order.
questions.question: Use brace expansion to print the sequence file_01 through file_05.
questions.description: {start..end} generates a sequence. {1..5} gives 1 2 3 4 5. {01..05} gives zero-padded 01 02 03 04 05. {a..z} gives the alphabet. Add a step: {0..10..2} gives 0 2 4 6 8 10.

questions.scenario: You want to match only files that start with a lowercase letter in /home/student/scripts.
questions.question: Use a character class glob to list files starting with any letter a-z in /home/student/scripts.
questions.description: [a-z] in a glob matches exactly one character in the range. [!abc] or [^abc] negates — matches any character NOT in the set. Character classes in globs are similar to regex but simpler and less powerful.
use_cases: Exclude files starting with numbers or underscores
hints: [a-z]* matches anything starting with a lowercase letter

questions.scenario: You need to quickly create a backup of a file by appending .bak, using brace expansion.
questions.question: Use brace expansion to copy /home/student/logs/app.log to app.log.bak in one cp command.
questions.description: cp file{,.bak} expands to cp file file.bak — the empty string before the comma means 'no suffix'. This is a very common brace expansion trick for backups. Much faster than typing the full path twice.
use_cases: Rename with a suffix without retyping the full path
hints: cp path{,.bak} expands to: cp path path.bak
The comma with nothing before it means empty string
